questions.stem: A multinational company has Snowflake accounts in AWS us-east-1, Azure West Europe, and GCP asia-southeast1. The cloud architecture team wants to understand which account-level features are available across all three cloud providers vs. those limited to specific clouds. Which Snowflake account feature is currently restricted to specific cloud providers or editions and NOT available uniformly across AWS, Azure, and GCP?

options.text: Multi-cluster warehouses
Tri-Secret Secure (customer-managed encryption keys)
Time Travel up to 90 days
Data sharing between accounts

questions.explanation: Tri-Secret Secure is correct because it requires Business Critical edition or higher AND is dependent on the cloud provider's KMS integration (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, or GCP Cloud KMS). While all three providers support TSS, the implementation details and availability can vary, and the feature requires explicit account configuration per cloud provider — it is not a default multi-cloud feature. Additionally, some Snowflake features have historically been rolled out first to AWS. Multi-cluster warehouses (A), Time Travel (C), and Data Sharing (D) are all generally available across AWS, Azure, and GCP at equivalent editions without cloud-specific restrictions.
questions.reference: Cloud provider feature parity: Tri-Secret Secure, Business Critical edition

options.text: SHOW ACCOUNTS requires the ACCOUNTADMIN role, not ORGADMIN

The ORGADMIN role only exists in one specific account in the organization and must be used from that account; using it in MYORG.DEV shows only MYORG.DEV unless ORGADMIN was originally configured in that account
SHOW ACCOUNTS only lists accounts in the same cloud region as the current account
The ORGADMIN role must be activated with SET ROLE = 'ORGADMIN' GLOBALLY before SHOW ACCOUNTS works

questions.explanation: B is correct because the ORGADMIN role, while conceptually organization-scoped, exists as a role within a single designated account. Only the account where ORGADMIN was enabled (typically the original account or a specifically designated account) will show all organization accounts when SHOW ACCOUNTS is run. Running SHOW ACCOUNTS from an account where ORGADMIN is not the 'home' ORGADMIN account will only show the current account. The ORGADMIN role must be used from the account where it has organization-level privileges. A is incorrect — ORGADMIN is the correct role for SHOW ACCOUNTS. C is incorrect — SHOW ACCOUNTS under ORGADMIN is not region-limited. D is incorrect — no such GLOBALLY syntax exists for SET ROLE.
questions.reference: ORGADMIN: single-account home, SHOW ACCOUNTS behavior

questions.stem: A company is migrating from a single-account Snowflake deployment to a multi-account organization structure. The architect must design when to use database replication versus data sharing for cross-account data access. Select TWO scenarios where database replication is MORE appropriate than data sharing.

options.text: The consuming account needs to run heavy analytical queries on large datasets and cannot accept compute latency from querying a remote account's storage
The consuming account is a reporting tool that needs always-current, real-time data with no acceptable staleness
The consuming account is a DR standby that must have a local copy of data in a different cloud region to survive provider-level outages
The consuming account needs read-only access to a reference dataset that changes infrequently (weekly updates)
Two business units need to share a master product catalog where the consumer can JOIN the catalog with their own local tables without network latency

questions.explanation: A is correct because when the consuming account needs to run compute-intensive analytical queries, replication provides a local copy that eliminates network latency between the consumer's warehouse and the provider's remote storage — shared data requires the consumer's warehouse to read from the provider's storage, which can introduce latency for large scans. C is correct because a DR standby in a different cloud region requires a physical copy of the data locally — data sharing does not copy data and so cannot satisfy DR requirements if the provider's cloud region is entirely unavailable. B is incorrect — data sharing provides always-current data (no refresh delay) making it superior for real-time access; replication introduces a refresh lag. D is incorrect — data sharing is ideal for infrequently changed reference data with no need to copy it. E is incorrect — JOINs with local tables work fine against shared data in Snowflake (the consumer's warehouse executes the join); replication is not required for this.
questions.reference: Replication vs data sharing: performance, DR, real-time access patterns

questions.stem: A company's ORGADMIN wants to enforce a standard set of parameters (like DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 30 and TIMEZONE = 'UTC') across all accounts in the organization. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Use ALTER ORGANIZATION SET PARAMETER = value to push parameters to all accounts simultaneously
Include account parameters in the Replication Group configuration so they replicate from a master account
Use an Infrastructure-as-Code tool (e.g., Terraform with the Snowflake provider) to manage account parameters consistently at account creation and via periodic drift detection
Submit a request to Snowflake Support to set organization-wide default parameters

questions.explanation: C is correct because Snowflake does not currently provide a native mechanism to push parameter changes to all accounts simultaneously from the ORGADMIN level. The recommended operational pattern is to manage account-level parameters through Infrastructure-as-Code tooling (Terraform, Pulumi, etc.) which can apply consistent configuration at account creation and detect/remediate drift over time. ALTER ORGANIZATION SET PARAMETER (A) is not valid Snowflake syntax for pushing parameters to all accounts. Replication Groups (B) do replicate some account-level settings as part of the ACCOUNT_PARAMETERS object type, but not all parameters and this is not the standard parameter governance approach. Snowflake Support (D) may set some parameters but this is not scalable or suitable for ongoing governance.
questions.reference: Account governance: Infrastructure-as-Code, Terraform Snowflake provider, parameter management

questions.stem: An architect is setting up a new Snowflake account in AWS eu-west-1 for a European subsidiary that must comply with GDPR data residency requirements. Which configuration ensures that Snowflake internal operations and metadata also remain within the EU?

options.text: Select the AWS eu-west-1 region when creating the account; Snowflake automatically ensures all data and metadata stays within the region
Enable Snowflake's GDPR mode using ALTER ACCOUNT SET GDPR_MODE = TRUE
Create the account in AWS eu-west-1, and additionally configure a Business Critical account to ensure Tri-Secret Secure keeps keys in the EU
Use a Virtual Private Snowflake (VPS) deployment in AWS eu-west-1 to guarantee all compute and metadata is fully isolated within the region

questions.explanation: A is correct because when a Snowflake account is created in a specific region (e.g., AWS eu-west-1), all customer data storage, compute execution, and metadata for that account remain within that region. Snowflake's architecture ensures regional data residency by default. GDPR_MODE (B) is not a real Snowflake parameter. While Tri-Secret Secure (C) is a valuable security control, it is not specifically required for data residency — the account region selection is what determines residency. VPS (D) provides dedicated infrastructure and is valuable for the highest isolation requirements, but regional data residency is guaranteed even for standard Snowflake accounts based on the region selection.
questions.reference: Data residency: regional accounts, GDPR compliance

questions.stem: A Snowflake ORGADMIN wants to enable an account named MYORG.ANALYTICS to create additional Snowflake accounts. Which statement describes this capability accurately?

options.text: Any account can create child accounts by granting ACCOUNTADMIN the CREATE ACCOUNT privilege
Only the account where the ORGADMIN role is held can create new accounts in the organization; the CREATE ACCOUNT privilege cannot be delegated to other accounts
Account creation can be delegated by granting the CREATE ACCOUNT privilege to an ORGADMIN role in MYORG.ANALYTICS
MYORG.ANALYTICS can create sub-accounts using CREATE SUBACCOUNT within its account context

questions.explanation: B is correct because account creation within a Snowflake organization is centralized — only the account designated as the ORGADMIN account can execute CREATE ACCOUNT. This privilege cannot be delegated to other accounts within the organization. This is an intentional architectural constraint to maintain centralized governance over account proliferation. A is incorrect — ACCOUNTADMIN manages objects within a single account and cannot create new accounts. C is incorrect — the ORGADMIN role exists in only one account and cannot be granted to other accounts as a cross-account privilege. D is incorrect — CREATE SUBACCOUNT is not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Organizations: account creation, ORGADMIN centralization

questions.stem: A data provider wants to share a dataset on the Snowflake Marketplace but wants to control which specific Snowflake accounts can see and request the listing, excluding competitors. Which listing configuration achieves this?

options.text: Create a public listing with a request-to-access workflow that manually approves each consumer
Create a Private Listing and specify the target Snowflake account(s) that are authorized to see and request the data
Create a Data Exchange listing within a private data exchange and invite only the desired accounts to join the exchange
Use a Personalized Listing with a consumer allowlist managed via ALLOWED_ACCOUNTS in the listing definition

questions.explanation: C is correct because a Private Data Exchange allows the provider to create a closed community of specific Snowflake accounts. Only invited accounts can see and access listings within the exchange — competitors cannot discover the exchange or its listings. This is the appropriate solution when the provider needs to share with a curated set of partners while keeping the data invisible to others. A public listing with manual approval (A) still makes the listing visible to everyone, including competitors — only access is gated. A Private Listing (B) can be sent to specific accounts, but the mechanism is slightly different from a Data Exchange (a private listing goes directly to named accounts). ALLOWED_ACCOUNTS (D) is not a valid listing definition parameter in Snowflake's current Data Exchange syntax.
questions.reference: Data Sharing: Private Data Exchange, listing visibility control

options.text: Tables cannot be shared from the same share that contains views referencing them
The view customer_summary is not a secure view; non-secure views cannot be included in shares, and the underlying tables cannot be added until the view is converted to a secure view
A share can contain either tables or views but not both simultaneously
The CUSTOMERS table is already implicitly shared as part of the view definition and cannot be explicitly added again

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake requires that views included in shares must be SECURE views (CREATE SECURE VIEW). A regular view exposes the view's SQL definition to the consumer, potentially revealing the underlying table structure and filter logic. If customer_summary is not a secure view, the GRANT SELECT will fail. Once the view is made secure, the provider can also grant SELECT on individual tables in the same share. A is incorrect — shares can contain both tables and views. C is incorrect — shares support a mix of object types. D is incorrect — sharing a view does not implicitly share the underlying tables; they are separate grants and must be explicitly added.
questions.reference: Data Sharing: secure views required in shares, GRANT SELECT on views

questions.stem: An architect is designing a data monetization strategy using Snowflake Marketplace. A financial data vendor wants to offer three tiers: (1) a free sample with 30 days of data, (2) a paid subscription with full history, and (3) a custom enterprise tier with enriched data. Select TWO architectural patterns that best support this tiered model natively on Snowflake Marketplace.

options.text: Create three separate shares — one per tier — each containing secure views that filter data appropriately (e.g., DATE >= DATEADD('day', -30, CURRENT_DATE()) for the free tier)
Create a single share with row access policies that dynamically filter data based on the consumer's account identifier and their contracted tier stored in a mapping table
Create three separate Marketplace listings, each pointing to its respective share, allowing consumers to subscribe to the appropriate tier
Create one Marketplace listing with tier selection managed via a query parameter passed at connection time
Use Snowgrid to deliver data to consumer accounts in their preferred region without creating separate shares

questions.explanation: A is correct because creating separate shares per tier with secure views enforcing data filters is the recommended pattern for tiered access. Each share exposes only what the tier permits — a secure view filtering to 30 days for the free tier, no filter for the full subscription tier, and a joined/enriched view for the enterprise tier. C is correct because each share should have its own Marketplace listing so consumers can discover and subscribe to their appropriate tier. The three listings link to their respective shares. B is a sophisticated alternative that works operationally but adds complexity — a single share with dynamic row access policies based on consumer account ID can work, but managing account-to-tier mappings requires ongoing maintenance and is harder to audit. D is incorrect — Snowflake connections do not support query parameters for data filtering at the connection level. E is incorrect — Snowgrid is not a data delivery mechanism for Marketplace tiers.
questions.reference: Marketplace: tiered data products, separate shares per tier, listing design

questions.stem: A provider uses Snowgrid to run a complex transformation on consumer-provided data without the consumer's raw data ever leaving their account. Which best describes how Snowgrid achieves this?

options.text: Snowgrid replicates the consumer's data to the provider's account, runs the transformation, then deletes the replica
Snowgrid allows the provider to execute compute (a warehouse or serverless task) in the consumer's account region against the consumer's data, returning only the results to the provider
Snowgrid creates a shared compute cluster that both provider and consumer contribute to equally
Snowgrid tunnels the consumer's data over an encrypted VPN to the provider's warehouse for processing

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowgrid (also known as cross-cloud business continuity and remote data execution) enables workloads to be executed on data where it lives. The provider's compute runs in the consumer's region against the consumer's data — the data does not move. Only the query results are returned to the provider's account. This is particularly valuable when data sovereignty or large data volumes make movement impractical. A is incorrect — Snowgrid specifically avoids copying consumer data to the provider. C is incorrect — Snowgrid does not create shared compute between parties. D is incorrect — no VPN tunneling occurs; Snowflake's internal network handles cross-account execution.
questions.reference: Snowgrid: cross-cloud workload execution, remote data processing

questions.stem: A consumer account mounts a share and creates: CREATE DATABASE shared_data FROM SHARE myorg.provider.sales_share; The consumer then tries to run: CREATE TABLE local_copy AS SELECT * FROM shared_data.public.transactions; What is the result and why does it matter architecturally?

options.text: The statement fails because consumers cannot run SELECT queries against shared databases
The statement succeeds and creates a full local copy of the shared data in the consumer's account storage, consuming the consumer's storage credits
The statement succeeds and creates a zero-copy clone of the shared data, consuming no additional storage
The statement is blocked because Snowflake prevents consumers from copying shared data to limit data exfiltration

questions.explanation: B is correct and has significant architectural implications. A consumer can run CTAS (CREATE TABLE AS SELECT) against shared data, which creates a full physical copy in the consumer's account storage — this consumes the consumer's storage credits and breaks the zero-copy nature of sharing. From a governance perspective, this means a provider who shares data cannot prevent the consumer from taking a permanent copy. Architecturally, this is why data providers should use secure views with time-limiting filters or other access controls in their shares — once data is shared, consumers can potentially extract it. A is incorrect — consumers can query shared data freely. C is incorrect — this is a CTAS, not a CLONE; it creates a full copy. D is incorrect — Snowflake does not block consumers from creating local copies of shared data.
questions.reference: Data sharing: consumer CTAS, data exfiltration risk, governance implications

questions.stem: A data provider wants to give a consumer access to a dataset on the Snowflake Marketplace. The provider has already created a share. What is the next step to make it available as a Marketplace listing?

options.text: Run GRANT SHARE TO PUBLIC to make the share discoverable on the Marketplace
Navigate to Provider Studio in Snowsight, create a listing, attach the share to the listing, and publish it to the Marketplace
Contact Snowflake Support to register the share as a Marketplace product and set the pricing model
Run ALTER SHARE ... SET MARKETPLACE = TRUE to publish the share automatically

questions.explanation: B is correct because the Snowflake Marketplace listing creation workflow happens entirely through Provider Studio in the Snowsight UI. The provider creates a listing, selects the share to attach, configures the listing details (description, title, pricing model), and publishes. GRANT SHARE TO PUBLIC (A) is not valid syntax — shares are added to accounts or listings, not granted to PUBLIC. Snowflake Support (C) is not required for standard Marketplace listings — the provider manages this self-service through Provider Studio. ALTER SHARE ... SET MARKETPLACE (D) is not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Marketplace: Provider Studio, listing creation, share attachment

questions.stem: An architect needs to ensure that after a failover from primary account MYORG.PROD_US to secondary account MYORG.PROD_DR, all applications connecting via JDBC continue working without configuration changes. The connection URL currently points to MYORG.PROD_US.snowflakecomputing.com. What must be configured to enable transparent application failover?

options.text: Configure a Failover Group and use the organization's connection URL (e.g., myorg.snowflakecomputing.com) in JDBC connections; client redirect automatically routes to the current primary
Configure a load balancer in front of both accounts; update the JDBC URL to point to the load balancer endpoint
Use a CNAME DNS entry pointing to the primary account; update it manually to the DR account when failover occurs
Configure Snowflake PrivateLink on both accounts; failover automatically updates the private endpoint DNS

questions.explanation: A is correct because Failover Groups support client redirect through an organization-level connection URL (myorg.snowflakecomputing.com). This URL always resolves to the current primary account. When the architect promotes the secondary to primary via ALTER FAILOVER GROUP ... PRIMARY, the organization connection URL automatically routes to the new primary. Applications using this URL require no reconfiguration. A load balancer (B) is not a Snowflake-supported pattern for failover — Snowflake's infrastructure is not load-balanced across accounts. A manually updated CNAME (C) would work but requires manual intervention during a disaster, contradicting the goal of transparent failover. PrivateLink (D) provides private network connectivity but does not automatically update DNS during failover.
questions.reference: Failover Groups: client redirect, organization connection URL

questions.stem:
A company creates the following:

CREATE FAILOVER GROUP prod_fg
  OBJECT_TYPES = DATABASES, WAREHOUSES, NETWORK POLICIES
  ALLOWED_DATABASES = analytics_db, raw_db
  ALLOWED_ACCOUNTS = MYORG.PROD_DR
  REPLICATION SCHEDULE = '10 MINUTE';

Select TWO statements that are accurate about this Failover Group configuration.

options.text: Network policies will be replicated to MYORG.PROD_DR, ensuring the DR account has the same IP allowlist/denylist rules as production
Data in analytics_db and raw_db will be replicated to MYORG.PROD_DR approximately every 10 minutes, giving an RPO of ~10 minutes
Resource monitors are automatically included because they are dependencies of the warehouses
Shares pointing to analytics_db or raw_db are included in this failover group because DATABASES implicitly includes associated shares
The REPLICATION SCHEDULE = '10 MINUTE' interval means the system attempts a refresh every 10 minutes but the actual RPO could be higher if a refresh fails

questions.explanation: A is correct because NETWORK POLICIES is explicitly listed in OBJECT_TYPES, so network policies are replicated. This is important for DR — without replicating network policies, the DR account would accept connections from all IP addresses. E is correct because the 10-minute schedule is a target interval; if a refresh fails (e.g., due to a large transaction volume or transient network issue), the next successful refresh determines the actual RPO, which could exceed 10 minutes. B is mostly correct regarding the approximate RPO but overstates certainty — see E. C is incorrect — resource monitors are NOT automatically included as warehouse dependencies; they must be explicitly specified in OBJECT_TYPES as RESOURCE MONITORS. D is incorrect — shares must be explicitly added as SHARES in OBJECT_TYPES; they are not implicitly included when databases are replicated.
questions.reference: Failover Groups: OBJECT_TYPES, NETWORK POLICIES, REPLICATION SCHEDULE, RPO

questions.stem: A company's DBA accidentally runs DROP TABLE orders PURGE in the production Snowflake account. The table had DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 set explicitly. Is recovery possible, and if so, how?

options.text: Recovery is not possible because DROP TABLE PURGE bypasses Fail-Safe and Time Travel; data is immediately and permanently deleted
Recovery is possible using UNDROP TABLE orders because UNDROP is always available for 24 hours regardless of the TIME_TRAVEL setting
Recovery is possible if a Failover Group refresh occurred within the last replication schedule interval; restore from the secondary account
Recovery is possible by contacting Snowflake Support who can retrieve data from Fail-Safe even for PURGE operations

questions.explanation: C is correct because when DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 is set, Time Travel is disabled and UNDROP will fail after the drop. The PURGE option in Snowflake's DROP TABLE means the data is not retained in Fail-Safe either (it bypasses the 7-day Fail-Safe period). However, if a Failover Group or Replication Group has replicated the database to a secondary account, and the last refresh captured the table before it was dropped, the data can be recovered from the secondary. A is the closest correct answer if no replication exists, but with a properly configured Failover Group, C applies. B is incorrect — UNDROP requires time travel; with retention = 0, the undrop window is effectively zero (the object is immediately removed from the internal history). D is incorrect — Snowflake Support cannot recover data from a PURGE operation even via Fail-Safe.
questions.reference: DR recovery: TIME_TRAVEL=0, DROP PURGE, Failover Group as recovery mechanism

questions.stem: An insurance company needs to meet a regulatory requirement that specifies: 'All production data must be recoverable within 4 hours with no more than 1 hour of data loss.' How should these requirements map to Snowflake DR design parameters?

options.text: RTO = 4 hours, RPO = 1 hour: Configure Failover Group with REPLICATION SCHEDULE = '60 MINUTE' and validate client redirect failover can complete in under 4 hours
RTO = 1 hour, RPO = 4 hours: Configure Time Travel with DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 1 to cover the 4-hour RPO window
RTO = 4 hours, RPO = 0: Use synchronous replication to achieve zero data loss
RTO = 1 hour, RPO = 1 hour: Configure a Replication Group with hourly refresh and document 1-hour switchover procedures

questions.explanation: A is correct. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is how quickly you must recover — 4 hours. RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is maximum acceptable data loss — 1 hour. A Failover Group with a 60-minute replication schedule achieves the 1-hour RPO. Client redirect ensures failover is triggered and applications reconnect without manual URL changes, contributing to meeting the 4-hour RTO. B maps the values incorrectly (RTO and RPO are swapped) and Time Travel does not provide cross-account DR. C is incorrect — Snowflake does not offer synchronous cross-account replication. D uses a Replication Group (not Failover Group), which does not support client redirect, making the 1-hour RTO harder to achieve — Failover Groups are the correct choice.
questions.reference: RPO/RTO: definition and mapping to Snowflake Failover Group configuration

questions.stem: What is the maximum Time Travel retention period available in Snowflake for a table in a Business Critical account?

options.text: 7 days
30 days
90 days
365 days

questions.explanation: 90 days is correct. Enterprise edition and above (including Business Critical and VPS) support a maximum Time Travel retention period of 90 days for tables. Standard edition is limited to 1 day. Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 90 on a table in a Business Critical account enables recovery of historical data for up to 90 days. 7 days (A) is the Fail-Safe period, not the maximum Time Travel period. 30 days (B) is between the standard maximum and the actual maximum. 365 days (D) exceeds the maximum supported retention period.
questions.reference: Time Travel: maximum retention period, Enterprise/Business Critical edition

questions.stem: An architect must document the difference between Replication Groups and Failover Groups for a DR runbook. Which statement accurately captures a key distinction?

options.text: Replication Groups support failover (account promotion) while Failover Groups only replicate data without the ability to change primary status

Failover Groups extend Replication Groups with the ability to change which account is primary and support client redirect; Replication Groups replicate data but cannot be promoted to replace a failed primary
Replication Groups replicate at the database level while Failover Groups replicate at the table level for more granular control
Failover Groups are only available for Enterprise edition; Replication Groups are available for all editions

questions.explanation: B is correct. Failover Groups are an extension of Replication Groups that add two critical DR capabilities: (1) the ability to execute ALTER FAILOVER GROUP ... PRIMARY to promote the secondary account to primary (changing read-write status), and (2) client redirect through the organization connection URL so applications automatically connect to the new primary. Replication Groups replicate data but are designed for read-scale and backup scenarios — they cannot be promoted and do not support client redirect. A has the relationship backwards. C is incorrect — both group types replicate at the database level, not different granularities. D is incorrect about edition requirements.
questions.reference: Replication Groups vs Failover Groups: failover capability, client redirect

questions.stem: An analyst runs the same parameterized dashboard query every 5 minutes. After the first run, subsequent executions complete in under 1 second. No data has changed in the underlying table. What Snowflake feature is responsible for this performance?

options.text: Automatic Clustering pre-fetches micro-partitions into memory for frequently accessed data
The Result Cache returns the previously computed result set without rerunning the query on the warehouse
The Local Disk Cache (warehouse cache) keeps recently scanned micro-partitions in SSD
Materialized views pre-compute and store the result of the dashboard query

questions.explanation: The Result Cache is correct because when the same query (identical SQL text) is submitted and the underlying data has not changed, Snowflake returns the cached result set immediately without consuming any warehouse compute credits. This is the 24-hour result cache that operates at the service layer, above the warehouse. Automatic Clustering (A) is a background maintenance process, not a query acceleration cache. The Local Disk Cache (C) stores micro-partition data on warehouse SSD to speed up scan operations — it still requires the warehouse to run. Materialized views (D) would require explicit creation by a user; the question describes an automatic behavior.
questions.reference: Result Cache: 24-hour retention, invalidation conditions, no warehouse credits

questions.stem: A query that runs on a Large warehouse consistently spills 200GB to local SSD (local spillage) and an additional 50GB to remote storage (remote spillage). Which remediation will have the greatest positive impact on query performance?

options.text: Enable Search Optimization Service on the tables being queried
Increase the warehouse size to XLarge or 2XLarge to provide more memory per node
Add a clustering key on the table to reduce micro-partition scanning
Enable result caching for the query

questions.explanation: B is correct because spillage (local and especially remote) indicates that the query's intermediate data exceeds the available memory on the warehouse nodes. Increasing the warehouse size provides more memory per node, reducing or eliminating spillage. Remote spillage (spilling to S3/Blob) is significantly more expensive performance-wise than local SSD spillage, so eliminating it has a dramatic impact. SOS (A) helps with equality predicate scan reduction but does not address memory pressure during aggregation. Clustering keys (C) reduce the number of micro-partitions scanned, which can reduce the volume of data processed and indirectly reduce spillage — but the direct fix for spillage is more memory (larger warehouse). Result caching (D) only applies to identical repeated queries and requires the warehouse to have run the query successfully first.
questions.reference: Performance: warehouse spillage, local vs remote spillage, warehouse sizing

questions.stem: An architect is designing a Materialized View (MV) strategy for a reporting workload on a 5TB fact table with daily refreshes. Select TWO conditions under which a Materialized View provides clear performance benefits over querying the base table directly.

options.text: Queries compute a SUM(revenue) GROUP BY product_category WHERE order_date >= DATEADD('day', -30, CURRENT_DATE())
Queries select individual rows by primary key: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_id = 12345
A complex multi-table JOIN that aggregates 3 fact tables and 5 dimension tables is run by 50 analysts hourly
A query applies a WHERE filter on a high-cardinality column with random access patterns, returning 0.001% of rows
A reporting view applies a window function (ROW_NUMBER OVER PARTITION BY...) on a 5TB table, run 100 times per day

questions.explanation: A is correct because aggregations (SUM, GROUP BY) on large tables are the primary use case for materialized views. The MV pre-computes the aggregate results and stores them; queries against the MV scan a fraction of the data. C is correct because complex multi-table joins with aggregations that are expensive to compute and run frequently are ideal for MVs — the MV stores the pre-joined, pre-aggregated result and refreshes it in the background. The 50 analysts × hourly cadence means the compute cost amortizes across many consumers. Point lookups by primary key (B) benefit from Search Optimization Service, not MVs — there is no aggregation to pre-compute. High-cardinality random access (D) is again an SOS use case. Window functions on large tables (E) could be put in an MV but window functions have specific restrictions in Snowflake MVs; more importantly, the result of a window function with CURRENT offsets may not be stable enough for MV caching.
questions.reference: Materialized Views: aggregation use cases, complex join pre-computation, refresh overhead

questions.stem: A team runs an ELT pipeline with these steps: (1) COPY INTO raw table, (2) INSERT INTO cleaned table with transformations, (3) MERGE INTO reporting table. Steps 1-3 are sequential and run on the same XLarge warehouse. The team wants to reduce total pipeline runtime. Which approach is most effective?

options.text: Split the three steps across three separate Large warehouses running simultaneously
Use a separate 2XLarge warehouse for the MERGE step which is typically the most resource-intensive operation
Enable multi-cluster mode on the XLarge warehouse so all three steps can run in parallel
Profile each step using EXPLAIN and query history, then right-size the warehouse for the most expensive step while considering task parallelism for independent steps

questions.explanation: D is correct because the steps are sequential (step 3 depends on step 2, which depends on step 1) so they cannot all run simultaneously (eliminating A and C as general solutions). The correct approach is to profile each step to identify the bottleneck: if the MERGE is 80% of the runtime, use a larger warehouse (e.g., 2XLarge) for that step and auto-suspend between steps. Splitting across three separate warehouses (A) doesn't help because the steps must run sequentially — three warehouses idle while one works is wasteful. Upgrading only the MERGE warehouse (B) makes sense if MERGE is the bottleneck, but without profiling data, this is a guess. D includes profiling as a prerequisite, which is architecturally sound.
questions.reference: ELT performance: sequential pipeline optimization, warehouse right-sizing, EXPLAIN

questions.stem: An architect is reviewing a query that runs for 47 minutes. The query profile shows: compilation time 2%, queue time 35%, execution time 63%. Execution breakdown: 45% scan, 40% sort, 15% aggregation. What should the architect investigate first to achieve the highest impact?

options.text: Optimize the sort operations by enabling Automatic Clustering on the sort key columns
Investigate and address the 35% queue time — this warehouse is under-provisioned for concurrent load; consider multi-cluster configuration
Increase the warehouse size to reduce the 45% scan time by adding more parallel scanners
Rewrite the query to eliminate the sort step using a different aggregation approach

questions.explanation: B is correct because 35% of the total time (approximately 16 minutes) is queue time — the query is waiting for the warehouse to have capacity before it even starts executing. No query optimization or warehouse size change helps with queuing. The right fix is to reduce queue time by either adding clusters (multi-cluster warehouse) or separating workloads. Only after addressing queuing should the architect tackle execution optimization. A (clustering for sort) addresses the sort step which is 40% of execution time = 40% of 63% = ~25% of total time, less impactful than the 35% queue time. C addresses scan time (~28% of total) — again less impactful than queuing. D is premature optimization before fixing the queuing issue.
questions.reference: Query profile analysis: queue time vs execution time, warehouse concurrency

questions.stem: An architect needs to choose between Standard and Economy scaling policies for a multi-cluster BI warehouse. The SLA requires that 95% of queries complete within 5 seconds. Historical data shows peak concurrency of 120 simultaneous queries for 20-minute bursts, twice daily. Which scaling policy is appropriate and why?

options.text: Economy, because the 20-minute burst duration justifies spinning up additional clusters and Economy policy optimizes for cost at any burst length
Standard, because the 5-second SLA requirement means queuing is unacceptable; Standard starts new clusters immediately when queries queue, meeting the latency SLA
Economy, because Snowflake's Economy policy predicts demand 10 minutes ahead and pre-warms clusters before the burst
Standard, because Economy policy only supports up to 2 clusters while Standard supports up to 10

questions.explanation: B is correct. Standard scaling policy starts additional clusters as soon as queries begin to queue, minimizing query wait time. With a 5-second SLA, any queue time is effectively a violation — Standard policy is mandatory. Economy policy (A) waits until Snowflake estimates a new cluster will stay busy for at least 6 minutes before starting it, accepting several minutes of queuing before a new cluster starts. This is incompatible with a 5-second SLA. C is incorrect — Economy policy does not pre-warm clusters based on demand prediction. D is incorrect — both Standard and Economy support up to the same MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT (configurable up to 10); the policy does not limit cluster count.
questions.reference: Multi-cluster: STANDARD vs ECONOMY scaling policy, SLA requirements

questions.stem: A company wants all Snowflake traffic from their Azure-hosted applications to use private network connectivity, bypassing the public internet. Which feature should the architect configure?

options.text: Configure a Network Policy to allow only the Azure VNet's private IP range
Configure Azure Private Link for Snowflake so that traffic flows over Microsoft's backbone network without traversing the internet
Enable Snowflake's VPN tunnel feature to encrypt traffic between the Azure VNet and Snowflake
Configure Snowflake's Internal Stage to keep data within Azure without internet exposure

questions.explanation: Azure Private Link for Snowflake is correct because it establishes a private endpoint in the customer's Azure VNet that connects directly to Snowflake's service over Microsoft's backbone network — no internet traversal occurs. This satisfies network isolation requirements for regulated environments. A Network Policy (A) filters by IP address but does not change the network path — traffic still goes over the internet. Snowflake does not have a VPN tunnel feature (C) — Private Link is the private connectivity mechanism. Internal Stages (D) are storage locations within Snowflake's cloud storage and are not a network connectivity feature.
questions.reference: Private Link: Azure Private Link, private network connectivity

questions.stem: An architect is implementing External OAuth with Okta for Snowflake access. Select TWO statements that are accurate about the External OAuth configuration in Snowflake.

options.text: The CREATE SECURITY INTEGRATION statement must specify the issuer URL, token endpoint URL, and jwks_uri for Okta JWT validation
Snowflake validates the OAuth token's signature and claims (expiry, audience, scope) using the external IdP's public keys
External OAuth replaces the need for Snowflake users to exist in the account — OAuth tokens create on-demand ephemeral users
The OAuth token must contain a claim that maps to a Snowflake role (e.g., a 'scp' or custom claim); the security integration must be configured with the claim mapping
External OAuth tokens are valid indefinitely once issued; Snowflake caches the token and does not re-validate with Okta on subsequent connections

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake validates OAuth JWT tokens by fetching the IdP's public keys (via the jwks_uri) and verifying the token's signature, issuer, audience, expiry, and scope claims. This is the standard OAuth 2.0 JWT validation flow. D is correct because External OAuth in Snowflake requires a role mapping — the OAuth token must contain a claim (like 'scp' for scope, or a custom claim configured in the security integration) that maps to a Snowflake role. If no valid role mapping exists, the authentication fails. A is partially correct but overspecified — the SECURITY INTEGRATION uses TYPE = OAUTH with the issuer and jwks_uri, but the exact parameters vary and the token_endpoint is not always required in the Snowflake config. C is incorrect — Snowflake users must still exist in the account for External OAuth; OAuth authenticates the user but the Snowflake user account must exist. E is incorrect — OAuth tokens have expiry claims that Snowflake validates; expired tokens are rejected.
questions.reference: External OAuth: security integration, token validation, role mapping

questions.stem: A company's DBA is creating a masking policy for a SALARY column. Employees should see their own salary (CURRENT_USER() matches the EMPLOYEE_ID), managers should see full salaries for their reports, and all others should see NULL. Which Snowflake function helps the masking policy correctly identify a manager's role regardless of secondary role activation?

options.text: CURRENT_ROLE()
INVOKER_ROLE()
IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION()
HAS_PRIVILEGE('ROLE', 'MANAGER_ROLE', 'USAGE')

questions.explanation: IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() is correct because it checks whether a specified role is currently active in the session, including roles inherited through the role hierarchy. A manager with MANAGER_ROLE granted (even through a parent role) will return TRUE from IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('MANAGER_ROLE') regardless of which specific role is set as the primary role. CURRENT_ROLE() (A) returns only the currently active (primary) role and misses users who may have the manager role through the hierarchy but have a different primary role set. INVOKER_ROLE() (B) returns the role of the caller in the context of an owner's rights procedure/function, not a general session role check. HAS_PRIVILEGE() (D) with this syntax is not valid Snowflake SQL.
questions.reference: Masking policies: IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() vs CURRENT_ROLE(), role hierarchy

questions.stem: An architect is reviewing a security design where a row access policy uses CURRENT_USER() to filter rows in a table containing 100 million rows. The policy maps users to allowed values in a lookup table of 50,000 rows. Performance tests show the policy causes a 10x slowdown on queries. Which optimization strategy should the architect recommend?

options.text: Replace CURRENT_USER() with CURRENT_ROLE() to reduce the lookup cardinality from users to roles
Pre-compute per-user row sets in a materialized view and reference the MV in the row access policy
Redesign the policy to use role-based filtering instead of user-based filtering, reducing the number of lookups from 50,000 users to a handful of roles, and map roles using IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION()
Enable the Search Optimization Service on the lookup table to accelerate the CURRENT_USER() lookup

questions.explanation: C is correct because user-based row access policies that query a 50,000-row lookup table on every row evaluation are expensive. Redesigning to role-based filtering dramatically reduces the cardinality of the mapping function — instead of 50,000 user entries, you might have 10 role entries. IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() is efficient for role checks. A is a simplification of this approach but CURRENT_ROLE() has the hierarchy limitation noted in other questions. B is architecturally interesting but materialized views in row access policies have restrictions in Snowflake and may not be supported. D would help with the lookup but SOS on a 50,000-row table provides marginal benefit — the real issue is the pattern of user-level mapping at row evaluation time.
questions.reference: Row Access Policy performance: role-based vs user-based filtering, IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION()

questions.stem: A Snowflake account administrator needs to automatically disable a Snowflake user account when the employee leaves the company. The company uses Azure Active Directory as its IdP. Which feature automates this process?

options.text: Configure a Snowflake task that runs ALTER USER ... SET DISABLED = TRUE on a schedule
Configure SCIM provisioning from Azure AD to Snowflake using a SCIM security integration; user deprovisioning in Azure AD automatically disables the Snowflake user
Configure External OAuth with Azure AD; revoking the OAuth token in Azure AD prevents Snowflake access
Configure SAML SSO; when the user is removed from Azure AD, they automatically lose Snowflake access on next session

questions.explanation: B is correct because SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) provides automated user lifecycle management. When an employee is deprovisioned in Azure AD, the SCIM protocol pushes a deactivation event to Snowflake, which immediately disables the user account. This is real-time and does not require manual intervention. A task on a schedule (A) introduces lag between HR action and access revocation — not suitable for security-critical offboarding. Revoking OAuth tokens (C) prevents new authentication but does not affect existing sessions and requires the user to have been using OAuth in the first place. SAML SSO (D) only prevents new logins (the next session attempt fails) but any currently active Snowflake sessions continue until they time out naturally.
questions.reference: SCIM: Azure AD integration, automated user deprovisioning

questions.stem: A company wants to reduce Snowflake storage costs. An architect identifies that the account holds 200TB of data with an average Time Travel retention of 30 days on all tables. Select TWO changes that would most directly reduce storage costs.

options.text: Run VACUUM on large tables to purge deleted records and reclaim storage
Reduce DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS from 30 to 7 days on large tables that do not require 30-day recovery windows
Convert large frequently-queried tables to transient tables to eliminate Fail-Safe storage overhead
Enable automatic compression by running ALTER TABLE ... SET COMPRESSION = AUTO on uncompressed tables
Partition large historical tables into smaller tables and drop tables containing data older than the business retention requirement

questions.explanation: B is correct because Time Travel storage (the historical versions of data) is billed in addition to current data storage. Reducing the retention period from 30 to 7 days eliminates 23 days' worth of historical storage on large tables — a significant reduction on 200TB. E is correct because dropping tables that contain data beyond the business retention requirement eliminates the storage cost of that data entirely, including its Time Travel and Fail-Safe storage. A is incorrect because Snowflake does not have a VACUUM command (unlike PostgreSQL/Databricks) — Snowflake's storage management is automatic and does not accumulate dead rows in the same way. C is incorrect — transient tables eliminate Fail-Safe but still have Time Travel storage; more importantly, converting large tables to transient loses the 7-day Fail-Safe safety net which may be unacceptable. D is incorrect — Snowflake automatically compresses data on ingestion using ZSTD or LZO; ALTER TABLE ... SET COMPRESSION = AUTO is for new file format definitions, not retroactive recompression of existing data.
questions.reference: Storage cost: Time Travel storage billing, table drops, retention period tuning

questions.stem: A Snowflake architect reviews a cost report and finds that Snowpipe is consuming 40% of monthly credits. The files loaded via Snowpipe are tiny (average 50KB) and arrive frequently (every 10 seconds). What is the primary cost driver and how should the architect address it?

options.text: Snowpipe charges per file loaded regardless of size; consolidate files into larger batches (at least 100-250MB) before loading to amortize the per-file overhead
Snowpipe charges per compute-second; the high frequency of tiny files means constant overhead credit consumption because each small file triggers a full warehouse spin-up
Snowpipe credit usage is fixed; the high cost indicates a configuration error where Snowpipe is retrying failed loads in an infinite loop
Enable Snowpipe's batch mode by setting BATCH_SIZE_LIMIT = '250MB' in the pipe definition to automatically coalesce tiny files

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowpipe's serverless cost model includes a fixed overhead per load request regardless of file size. Loading thousands of 50KB files means paying thousands of small overhead charges. The Snowflake best practice recommendation is to stage files until they are at least 100-250MB before triggering a Snowpipe load. This dramatically reduces the number of load operations and therefore the credit consumption. B is incorrect — Snowpipe is serverless and does not use warehouse compute; there is no 'warehouse spin-up.' Snowpipe uses Snowflake's serverless infrastructure and bills per credit-second of serverless usage, but the per-file overhead is the primary issue. C is incorrect — there is no 'batch mode' configuration in Snowpipe with a BATCH_SIZE_LIMIT parameter. BATCH_SIZE_LIMIT does not exist in the pipe DDL.
questions.reference: Snowpipe cost optimization: file size best practices, per-file overhead, 100-250MB recommendation

questions.stem: An architect is reviewing SHOW PARAMETERS IN ACCOUNT output and sees NETWORK_POLICY = 'CORP_NET' at the account level. A newly deployed application service account, SVCAPP, needs to connect from an IP address not in CORP_NET's allowlist. Rather than modifying the account-wide policy, what is the most targeted fix?

options.text: Create a new network policy with the expanded IP list and replace CORP_NET at the account level
Run ALTER USER SVCAPP SET NETWORK_POLICY = SVC_POLICY where SVC_POLICY allows the application IP; the user-level policy overrides the account-level policy only for SVCAPP
Add the application IP to the BLOCKED_IP_LIST exclusion list in CORP_NET
Create a separate Snowflake account for the application so it inherits no network policy

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake's network policy precedence allows user-level policies to override account-level policies for that specific user. By assigning a dedicated SVC_POLICY to SVCAPP that includes the application IP, only SVCAPP gets the expanded access — all other users continue to be governed by CORP_NET. This is the targeted, least-privilege approach. A replaces the account-wide policy, expanding access for all users — a broad change that may violate security requirements. C is incorrect because BLOCKED_IP_LIST contains IPs to explicitly deny, not to allow; you cannot use an exclusion from the block list to grant access to addresses not in the allow list. D is an extreme measure that creates operational overhead and is unnecessary.
questions.reference: Network Policies: user-level policy assignment, ALTER USER SET NETWORK_POLICY, precedence

questions.stem: An architect needs to understand the scope of Snowflake parameter inheritance when a session-level parameter is set. A user runs SET TIMEZONE = 'America/Los_Angeles' in their session. The account-level TIMEZONE is 'UTC'. Which statement is correct about the effective TIMEZONE for this user's session queries?

options.text: 'UTC' — account-level parameters always override session-level settings
'America/Los_Angeles' — session-level SET commands create the highest-priority override, taking precedence over account, database, schema, and object-level parameters
The SET command fails because only ACCOUNTADMIN can change TIMEZONE
'America/Los_Angeles' only for the first query; subsequent queries reset to the account default

questions.explanation: B is correct because the Snowflake parameter hierarchy from lowest to highest priority is: Snowflake defaults → account-level → database-level → schema-level → object-level → session-level. A session-level SET command is the highest override and governs all queries within that session. When the user runs SET TIMEZONE = 'America/Los_Angeles', all timestamp functions and conversions in that session use the Pacific time zone. A is incorrect — session overrides the account. C is incorrect — any user can run SET for session parameters; ACCOUNTADMIN is not required. D is incorrect — session parameters persist for the entire session until changed again or the session ends.
questions.reference: Parameter inheritance hierarchy: session-level as highest override, SET command

questions.stem: A company's security audit finds that ACCOUNTADMIN is used by 12 different data engineers for daily ETL tasks. The audit recommends restricting ACCOUNTADMIN to fewer than 3 named individuals. To audit ongoing ACCOUNTADMIN usage going forward, which ACCOUNT_USAGE view should the architect query?

options.text: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered by ROLE_NAME = 'ACCOUNTADMIN'
ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY filtered by REPORTED_CLIENT_TYPE = 'ACCOUNTADMIN'
ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_USERS where ROLE = 'ACCOUNTADMIN'
ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS filtered by AUTHENTICATION_METHOD = 'ACCOUNTADMIN'

questions.explanation: A is correct because ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY includes the ROLE_NAME column, which captures the active role at the time each query was executed. Filtering by ROLE_NAME = 'ACCOUNTADMIN' shows every query run under the ACCOUNTADMIN role — who ran it, when, what SQL, and from which warehouse. This provides a complete audit trail of ACCOUNTADMIN operational usage. ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY (B) tracks authentication events but does not record which role was active during query execution after login. GRANTS_TO_USERS (C) shows who has been granted ACCOUNTADMIN but not when or how often they used it. SESSIONS (D) — there is no AUTHENTICATION_METHOD = 'ACCOUNTADMIN' filter; authentication method refers to the login mechanism, not the role.
questions.reference: ACCOUNTADMIN audit: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY, ROLE_NAME filter

questions.stem: An architect is implementing budget governance using Snowflake's native Budget feature. A custom budget is created to monitor a specific group of 5 warehouses used by the data science team. The budget limit is set to 500 credits per month. At 480 credits consumed, the data science team's lead warehouse is suspended by a Resource Monitor. What is the behavior of the Budget at this point?

options.text: The Budget automatically suspends all other warehouses in the budget group once the 500-credit limit is reached

The Budget continues to track and alert on spending but does not suspend warehouses — enforcement is the Resource Monitor's role; the Budget will send an alert notification when projected spend exceeds 500 credits
The Budget takes over from the Resource Monitor once the Resource Monitor suspends a warehouse; the Budget resets the Resource Monitor's counter
Budget alerts are suppressed when a Resource Monitor is active on the same warehouses to prevent duplicate notifications

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake Budgets and Resource Monitors serve complementary but distinct roles. Resource Monitors are the enforcement mechanism — they can SUSPEND or SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY a warehouse when credit thresholds are hit. Budgets are observability tools — they monitor projected spending across a group of objects and send alert notifications when spending is on pace to exceed the budget limit. A Budget never directly suspends a warehouse; that is exclusively the Resource Monitor's job. The two features coexist and complement each other: the Resource Monitor enforces hard limits, the Budget provides spending visibility and early warning. A is incorrect — Budgets have no warehouse suspension capability. C is incorrect — Budgets and Resource Monitors operate independently; no counter reset occurs. D is incorrect — Budgets and Resource Monitors send separate notifications through different channels.
questions.reference: Budgets vs Resource Monitors: observability vs enforcement, complementary roles

questions.stem: A company wants to migrate from account locators (e.g., 'ab12345') to the organization account name format (e.g., 'myorg.prod_main') for all connection strings. Select TWO considerations that the architect must address during this migration.

options.text: All JDBC, ODBC, Python connector, and SnowSQL connection strings must be updated to reference the new org.account format; account locators continue to work in parallel during transition
Database replication configurations that reference account locators in the ALLOWED_ACCOUNTS clause must be updated to use the org.account format
Account locators must be explicitly decommissioned using ALTER ACCOUNT DROP LOCATOR before the org.account format becomes active
Data sharing ADD ACCOUNTS statements and any consumer-side database references should be reviewed to ensure they use the org.account format for new shares
The org.account format requires Business Critical edition; Standard edition accounts must upgrade before migrating connection strings

questions.explanation: A is correct because the practical migration task is updating all client connection strings to use the org.account format. Crucially, both formats work simultaneously — the account locator does not stop working when the org.account format is adopted. This allows a phased migration. D is correct because data sharing GRANT and ADD ACCOUNTS commands, plus consumer-side CREATE DATABASE FROM SHARE references, should use the org.account format for consistency and clarity. Old locator-based shares continue to work but are harder to audit and manage. B is incorrect — replication configurations use account names in the ALLOWED_ACCOUNTS clause and already support the org.account format, but account locators also work there; updating them is good practice but not a strict requirement for migration. C is incorrect — there is no ALTER ACCOUNT DROP LOCATOR command; account locators cannot be decommissioned. E is incorrect — the org.account format is available across all Snowflake editions.
questions.reference: Account identifier migration: account locator to org.account, connection string updates, data sharing

questions.stem: A company's platform team uses SYSTEM$SHOW_BUDGETS() to list all active budgets in their account. The function returns two budgets: one named 'ACCOUNT_BUDGET' and one named 'DS_TEAM_BUDGET'. What distinguishes these two budget types in Snowflake's Budget framework?

options.text: ACCOUNT_BUDGET is a system-managed budget that Snowflake automatically creates for all accounts; DS_TEAM_BUDGET is a user-created budget
ACCOUNT_BUDGET is the account-level budget that monitors all credit-consuming resources in the account; DS_TEAM_BUDGET is a custom budget scoped to a specific group of objects chosen by the user
ACCOUNT_BUDGET is read-only and cannot be modified; DS_TEAM_BUDGET can be adjusted by any user with SYSADMIN
Both are identical budget objects; the name difference is purely cosmetic and does not affect behavior

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake's Budget framework has two types: (1) the account budget — one per account, monitors all credit consumption across the entire account — and (2) custom budgets — user-created, scoped to a specific set of objects (e.g., specific warehouses, databases, or serverless features) that the user groups together for monitoring. ACCOUNT_BUDGET is the account-wide monitor; DS_TEAM_BUDGET is a custom budget scoped to the data science team's resources. Both types appear in SYSTEM$SHOW_BUDGETS() output. A is incorrect — ACCOUNT_BUDGET is not automatically created by Snowflake; it is created by the user but represents the account scope. C is incorrect — the account budget can be modified by authorized roles. D is incorrect — the scope difference is fundamental, not cosmetic.
questions.reference: Budget types: account budget vs custom budget, SYSTEM$SHOW_BUDGETS()

questions.stem: A data provider shares a view that references a table in a second database (finance_db) using GRANT REFERENCE_USAGE ON DATABASE finance_db TO SHARE analytics_share. A consumer queries the shared view and receives results. Six months later, the provider's DBA drops finance_db. What happens to the consumer's queries against the shared view?

options.text: Consumer queries continue to work because REFERENCE_USAGE caches the referenced data for 90 days
Consumer queries fail immediately with an error because the view's underlying referenced database no longer exists; the view becomes invalid
Snowflake automatically reroutes the view's reference to the closest available replica of finance_db
The shared view continues to work for 7 days (Fail-Safe period) after finance_db is dropped, then fails

questions.explanation: B is correct because the shared view is a live object that references real tables in finance_db. When finance_db is dropped, the underlying tables the view depends on no longer exist. Any consumer query against the shared view will receive an error because Snowflake's query engine cannot resolve the cross-database reference. The GRANT REFERENCE_USAGE grant becomes meaningless when the referenced database is gone. This is an important operational risk: a provider who drops or restructures a referenced database breaks dependent shared views without any automatic notification to consumers. A is incorrect — there is no caching of cross-database references in data sharing. C is incorrect — there is no automatic rerouting; Snowflake does not manage replicas for this purpose. D is incorrect — Fail-Safe applies to dropped data recovery, not to view reference availability.
questions.reference: REFERENCE_USAGE: cross-database view sharing, dependency on referenced database availability

questions.stem: An architect is designing a Snowflake Data Clean Room for two insurance companies to collaborate on fraud detection. Select TWO characteristics of Snowflake Data Clean Room templates that enforce privacy constraints.

options.text: Templates restrict the types of joins allowed — for example, only allowing inner joins on agreed identifier columns, preventing consumers from using cross joins to enumerate all rows
Templates enforce minimum aggregation thresholds — for example, requiring COUNT(*) >= 100 before returning any result, preventing identification of rare individuals
Templates encrypt individual row values with the consumer's public key before returning results, ensuring row-level privacy
Templates are immutable SQL objects stored in Snowflake's system tables; they cannot be modified by either party after agreement
Templates allow only the pre-defined aggregation and join patterns; ad-hoc query modifications outside the template are blocked

questions.explanation: B is correct because minimum aggregation thresholds (k-anonymity constraints) are a core privacy enforcement mechanism in Data Clean Rooms. By requiring results to represent at least a minimum group size, the clean room prevents a party from issuing queries that isolate a single individual's record and inferring sensitive attributes. E is correct because the fundamental privacy enforcement in Snowflake Data Clean Rooms is template restriction — only the pre-defined query templates can be executed. A party cannot deviate from the template to run arbitrary SQL; the clean room architecture physically prevents exploratory queries outside the approved templates. A describes a valid constraint within a template (restricting join types), but the privacy mechanism is the template itself (E), not specifically join-type restriction as a standalone feature. C is incorrect — Data Clean Rooms do not perform row-level encryption with consumer keys. D is incorrect — templates are Snowflake objects that can be updated by the provider; the governance around mutability is procedural, not enforced by Snowflake making them immutable.
questions.reference: Data Clean Rooms: template restriction, minimum aggregation threshold, privacy enforcement

questions.stem: A data provider has published a versioned conceptual share — a logical construct where the provider intends to maintain stable interfaces across schema changes. The provider updates the underlying secure view's logic (adding a new computed column) but keeps the same view name. What does the consumer experience?

options.text: The consumer must re-mount the share (re-run CREATE DATABASE FROM SHARE) to pick up the view changes
The consumer's next query against the shared view automatically includes the new computed column because shares are live references to the provider's objects
The consumer receives a change notification email from Snowflake and must approve the new schema before the column appears
The new column is invisible until the provider runs ALTER SHARE ... REFRESH to propagate the view definition change

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake data sharing is live — the consumer database created from the share is a pointer to the provider's actual objects. When the provider updates the secure view's definition (ALTER VIEW ... AS ...), the next consumer query automatically executes against the new view definition. There is no re-mounting required and no consumer approval step. This is one of the key operational advantages of data sharing over data replication — providers can evolve their data products and consumers immediately benefit from improvements without any synchronization action. A is incorrect — re-mounting is not required; the share reference is always live. C is incorrect — there is no change notification or approval mechanism for view definition updates. D is incorrect — ALTER SHARE ... REFRESH is not a valid Snowflake command; no provider-side refresh is required for view definition changes.
questions.reference: Data Sharing: live share references, view definition updates immediately visible to consumers

questions.stem: A provider wants to query consumer-side data from within the provider's Snowflake account using Snowgrid, without the consumer's raw data being copied to the provider. The provider's data science team needs to run a JOIN between the provider's enrichment table and the consumer's transaction table. Which Snowgrid architectural element makes this possible?

options.text: The provider creates a share of their enrichment table to the consumer, and the consumer creates a share of their transaction table back to the provider; the JOIN runs on neutral third-party compute

The provider creates an inbound share from the consumer's account; the provider's warehouse reads consumer data via the shared database and JOINs with the provider's local enrichment table, running entirely on the provider's warehouse
Snowgrid provisions dedicated cross-cloud compute in a neutral cloud region that has access to both parties' storage
The consumer grants the provider EXECUTE privilege on a Stored Procedure that performs the JOIN and returns only aggregate results

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowgrid for cross-account data access works through the standard data sharing mechanism from the consumer's perspective: the consumer shares their transaction table to the provider's account. The provider then mounts it as an inbound share (CREATE DATABASE FROM SHARE). The provider's warehouse can then JOIN the shared (live, consumer-side) transaction table with the provider's local enrichment table. The consumer's raw data never moves — the provider's warehouse reads micro-partitions from the consumer's storage across accounts. The JOIN results are returned to the provider's warehouse output. A is incorrect — no neutral third-party compute exists; the provider's warehouse executes the query. C is incorrect — Snowgrid doesn't provision separate neutral-region compute; it uses the existing account-to-account sharing model. D while achievable, is a different pattern (consumer-controlled stored procedure) not specifically the Snowgrid model.
questions.reference: Snowgrid: inbound share JOIN, provider warehouse queries consumer shared data

questions.stem: A data provider runs SHOW SHARES and sees two entries for 'SALES_SHARE': one with kind=OUTBOUND and one with kind=INBOUND. What does the INBOUND entry represent in this context?

options.text: An error condition — a share cannot appear as both INBOUND and OUTBOUND in the same account
The INBOUND entry represents a share that another organization has sent back to this account, perhaps as part of a bidirectional data exchange
The INBOUND entry represents the same SALES_SHARE as seen from the consumer's perspective; Snowflake shows both sides in the provider's SHOW SHARES for auditing
The INBOUND entry means a different data provider has shared a dataset also named SALES_SHARE to this account; name collisions are allowed between inbound and outbound shares

questions.explanation: D is correct because SHOW SHARES in a Snowflake account lists both outbound shares the account created (kind=OUTBOUND) and inbound shares from other providers that this account has been granted access to (kind=INBOUND). If another provider happens to have named their share SALES_SHARE, it will appear as an INBOUND entry with the same name as the provider's own OUTBOUND share. The shares are distinct objects — they differ in kind, provider, and the full qualified name includes the source account. A is incorrect — there is no error; both can coexist with the same name as long as they are of different kinds. B describes the scenario accurately in terms of direction but implies reciprocity that may not exist. C is incorrect — Snowflake does not show the consumer's view in the provider's account.
questions.reference: SHOW SHARES: INBOUND vs OUTBOUND, name collisions, qualified share identity

questions.stem: A provider runs SHOW LISTING in their account to audit their Marketplace presence. The output shows 3 listings. One listing has STATUS = 'UNPUBLISHED'. Which of the following is true about this unpublished listing?

options.text: UNPUBLISHED means the listing was published then revoked by Snowflake for policy violation; it cannot be republished by the provider

UNPUBLISHED means the listing is in draft state or has been taken down by the provider; consumers cannot discover or access it, but the underlying share still grants access to any previously approved consumers
UNPUBLISHED means Snowflake's review process rejected the listing; it requires Snowflake Support intervention to change status
UNPUBLISHED listings are completely inactive — all consumer access including previously approved consumers is immediately revoked

questions.explanation: B is correct because UNPUBLISHED in Snowflake Marketplace means the listing is not publicly discoverable — new consumers cannot find or request it. However, the share object itself is separate from the listing. If the provider previously approved specific consumers and those consumers have already mounted the share (CREATE DATABASE FROM SHARE), their access is controlled by the share grant (ALTER SHARE ADD ACCOUNTS), not by the listing status. Unpublishing a listing removes discoverability but does not revoke existing share grants. A is incorrect — UNPUBLISHED can be a draft state or provider-initiated takedown, not necessarily a policy violation. C is incorrect — UNPUBLISHED status is managed by the provider in Provider Studio, not by Snowflake's review process. D is incorrect — unpublishing a listing does not revoke share access to existing consumers.
questions.reference: Marketplace listings: SHOW LISTING, UNPUBLISHED status, listing vs share independence

questions.stem: A company runs a Failover Group with REPLICATION_SCHEDULE = '15 MINUTE'. The business calculates that their worst-case RPO over the past quarter is 47 minutes — far exceeding the 15-minute target. Which two root causes should the architect investigate first?

options.text: The secondary account's region is too far from the primary, causing high network latency that slows replication

Large transaction volumes during the refresh window are causing individual refreshes to take longer than 15 minutes, so consecutive refreshes are separated by the refresh duration rather than the schedule interval
The warehouse assigned to the Failover Group is too small and needs to be upgraded to 4XLarge to handle replication volume
Intermittent refresh failures — if a refresh fails and retries, the effective gap between successful refreshes can be a multiple of the schedule interval

questions.explanation: Both B and D are correct root causes for RPO exceeding the configured schedule. B: If individual refresh operations take longer than 15 minutes (due to high DML volume on the primary), each refresh completes after its predecessor, creating a gap equal to the refresh duration, not the schedule. A 30-minute refresh = 30-minute RPO. D: If a refresh fails (transient network issue, primary resource contention), the system retries but the failed refresh creates a gap. If two consecutive refreshes fail, the gap becomes 2× the schedule. An architect investigating this pattern should examine the replication history (REPLICATION_GROUP_REFRESH_HISTORY) for failed and slow refreshes. A is not a primary concern — Snowflake's global backbone minimizes cross-region latency for replication; it is a minor factor. C is incorrect — Failover Group replication uses Snowflake's internal serverless infrastructure, not a customer-managed warehouse.
questions.reference: Failover Groups: RPO variance, refresh duration exceeding schedule, failed refresh impact

questions.stem: After a failover from MYORG.PROD to MYORG.PROD_DR, the team needs to verify the data state on the new primary before declaring the failover complete. Which approach provides the most reliable validation that the promoted secondary has the correct data?

options.text: Run COUNT(*) on key tables in MYORG.PROD_DR and compare to the last known row counts from MYORG.PROD

Query ACCOUNT_USAGE.REPLICATION_GROUP_REFRESH_HISTORY on MYORG.PROD_DR to confirm the last successful refresh timestamp, then validate business-critical table row counts and checksums against pre-failover benchmarks
Run SHOW DATABASES on MYORG.PROD_DR to confirm all databases are present and accessible
Check Snowflake's status page to confirm replication completed successfully before the outage

questions.explanation: B is correct because a thorough post-failover validation requires two things: (1) confirming the timestamp of the last successful refresh from REPLICATION_GROUP_REFRESH_HISTORY — this tells you exactly how far back the promoted secondary's data is, and (2) running business-critical data quality checks (row counts, checksums, spot-check queries on key entities) against pre-documented benchmarks. Together, these give the architect confidence about data completeness and correctness. A (row counts only) is a useful check but insufficient alone — row counts could match while specific records are missing or corrupted. C (SHOW DATABASES) only confirms the database objects exist, not their data integrity. D (Snowflake status page) provides service health information but does not validate specific replication completion status for a particular Failover Group.
questions.reference: Post-failover validation: REPLICATION_GROUP_REFRESH_HISTORY, data integrity checks

questions.stem: A company's disaster recovery plan requires them to execute ALTER DATABASE analytics_db PRIMARY on the consumer (secondary) account. What must be true for this command to succeed?

options.text: The database must be part of a Failover Group (not just a Replication Group) and the secondary account must have the ACCOUNTADMIN role executing the command
The database can be promoted independently using ALTER DATABASE ... PRIMARY without requiring a Failover Group configuration
The command requires Snowflake Support to pre-authorize the failover before ALTER DATABASE PRIMARY can be executed
ALTER DATABASE ... PRIMARY is only valid when the primary account is completely unreachable; it fails if the primary is still online

questions.explanation: A is correct because database-level promotion (ALTER DATABASE ... PRIMARY) is only supported when the database is part of a Failover Group — not a simple Replication Group. Failover Groups are specifically designed to support the promotion of a secondary to primary. The command must be executed by a role with sufficient privileges (ACCOUNTADMIN or a role with the appropriate privilege) on the secondary account. B is incorrect — ALTER DATABASE ... PRIMARY only works for databases that are members of a Failover Group. C is incorrect — no Snowflake Support pre-authorization is required for a standard failover; it is a self-service operation. D is incorrect — planned failovers (where the primary is online and healthy) are fully supported and are the recommended way to test DR procedures; the command does not require the primary to be unreachable.
questions.reference: ALTER DATABASE PRIMARY: Failover Group requirement, planned vs emergency failover

questions.stem: A company performs business continuity testing by running a full failover and failback. Select TWO items from the DR runbook that must be manually executed AFTER the secondary account is promoted to primary, before declaring the failover complete.

options.text: Resume all Snowflake Tasks that were suspended during replication — tasks arrive on the new primary in SUSPENDED state and must be explicitly resumed with ALTER TASK ... RESUME
Re-grant all data shares — shares are not replicated and must be recreated from scratch on the new primary
Recreate all warehouses — warehouses are not replicated as part of Failover Groups
Update application DNS or verify the organization connection URL is routing correctly to the newly promoted primary account
Re-create all Resource Monitors — they are never replicated regardless of OBJECT_TYPES configuration

questions.explanation: A is correct because Tasks replicated via a Failover Group arrive on the secondary in SUSPENDED state. After failover, they must be manually resumed with ALTER TASK ... RESUME. Failing to do this means scheduled data pipelines will not run on the new primary. D is correct because even with client redirect configured, the team should verify the organization connection URL is correctly routing to the new primary before declaring the failover complete — this can be checked by connecting via the org URL and confirming it reaches the new primary account. B is incorrect — if SHARES is included in the Failover Group OBJECT_TYPES, shares ARE replicated; they do not need to be recreated. C is incorrect — if WAREHOUSES is in OBJECT_TYPES, they are replicated. E is incorrect — Resource Monitors can be replicated if RESOURCE MONITORS is specified in OBJECT_TYPES.
questions.reference: Post-failover runbook: task resumption, connection URL verification, replicated objects
